text: In the operating room, the surgeon retracts the colon with their left hand while carefully detaching loops of small bowel tethered in the pelvis. The surgeon addresses serosal tears on the small bowel loops, visible with stitches.

The surgeon retracts the colon with the left hand while carefully detaching loops of small bowel from pelvic adhesions. Visible stitches are present, and the surgeon repairs serosal tears on the small bowel.
The surgeon examines the fused sigmoid mesentery attached to the retroperitoneum over the sacral promontory, marking the area for the planned resection.
The surgeon examines the fused sigmoid mesentery attached to the retroperitoneum over the sacral promontory, marking the area for the planned resection.
The surgeon begins the dissection laterally, pressing on the superior aspect of the stent and continuing the dissection down towards the dome of the bladder.

The surgeon begins lateral dissection, pressing on the superior aspect of the stent and carefully dissecting down to the dome of the bladder. Dense adhesions are visible between the colon, stent, and bladder, resembling a perforated diverticulitis case with a colovesical fistula.

The surgeon attempts to dissect laterally around the stent, using ureteral catheters and ICG for guidance. The colon is adhered to the bladder, and the surgeon carefully addresses these adhesions. Visible wires of the stent are noted, suggesting necrosis or perforation into the bladder.

The surgeon uses ureteral catheters and ICG to ensure the ureter and bladder are intact while directly addressing the adherence of the colon to the bladder. The wires of the stent are visible during this process.

The surgeon navigates below the inflammatory reaction surrounding the stent, aiming to create a new wide open anastomosis. The tissue appears inflamed, indicating possible necrosis or perforation of the stent into the bladder.

The surgeon navigates below the inflammatory reaction to create a new wide open anastomosis, carefully considering the potential challenges of reaching the proximal colon due to previous resections and mobilizations.
The surgeon navigates down the right side, revealing the stent that has eroded through the tissue. They carefully dissect to find a plane above the colon wall, avoiding perforation of the bladder.

The surgeon dissects down the right side, revealing the area where the stent has eroded through. They carefully navigate to establish a plane above the colon wall, avoiding perforation of the bladder, and begin to identify a proper dissection plane.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to navigate dense fibrotic adhesions, finding a plane into the pelvis. The colon is being carefully dissected anteriorly off the bladder, following surgical principles of working from easy to challenging areas.

The surgeon utilizes robotic instruments to navigate dense fibrotic adherence between the colon and bladder, seeking a surgical plane in the pelvis. The robotic tools provide strength and tensile firmness, aiding in the dissection process as the surgeon works on the opposite side.
The surgeon carefully dissects the colon from the anterior surface of the bladder, following surgical principles to separate the dense fibrotic adherence between these structures.

The surgeon navigates the dissection between the bladder wall and the anterior wall of the colon, aiming to reach the normal rectum below the stent level. The surgeon identifies the mesorectum and locates the proper dissection plane for planning the resection.

The surgeon navigates the surgical field, briefly entering the bladder wall before returning to the anterior wall of the colon. The dissection continues deep enough to reveal normal rectum tissue beneath, ensuring it is below the level of the stent. The surgeon identifies the mesorectum as a wad of fat and locates the proper dissection plane.

The surgeon clears the mesentery to the mid rectum, ensuring it is taken right up to the bowel wall to prepare a smooth surface for the stapler. The splenic flexure is remobilized to achieve a tension-free anastomosis, and the mesentery is dissected below the stent seated in the mid rectum.
The surgeon clears the mesentery to the mid rectum, ensuring to get below the stent seated in the mid rectum. The splenic flexure has been remobilized to facilitate a tension-free anastomosis.

The surgeon carefully approaches the bowel wall to prepare a clean surface for the stapler, ensuring there is no bleeding. To confirm the absence of the stent before stapling, the anterior wall of the rectum is opened, revealing the distal aspect of the stent.

The surgeon opens the anterior wall of the rectum to visually confirm the distal extent of the stent before stapling. The stapler is applied across the rectum, requiring an additional staple to complete the transection. The proximal margin is cleaned up, located just proximal to the stent, without needing further opening of the bowel.

The surgeon uses a stapler to transect the bowel, requiring an additional staple to completely cross the tissue. The proximal margin is cleaned up, located just proximal to the stent, which is palpable, allowing the surgeon to avoid opening this section of the bowel before dividing it.

The surgeon performs an anastomosis by joining two staple lines using a 3-0 V-Loc suture, gently bringing the two ends together. The patient is kept in a steep Trendelenburg position to prevent the small bowel from filling the pelvis, ensuring a tension-free connection.

The surgeon performs an anastomosis by joining two staple lines using a 3-0 V-Loc suture, gently bringing the two ends together under tension. The patient remains in steep Trendelenburg position to keep the small bowel out of the pelvis, facilitating the procedure.

The surgeon joins the two posterior staple lines to perform a two-layer anastomosis. A proctotomy is made while the first robotic arm holds the small bowel in the right lower quadrant, pressed against two sponges to keep it out of the surgical field.

The surgeon performs a two-layer anastomosis, joining the posterior staple lines and creating a proctotomy. The camera and two working arms are used, with the first arm holding the small bowel in the right lower quadrant, pressed against sponges to keep it out of the way. The inner layer of the anastomosis is completed by adjoining the lip between the otomy and the posterior staple lines, with stitches placed precisely. The surgeon checks tension and begins the second inner suture, ensuring good exposure and stitch placement.

The surgeon performs the inner layer of the anastomosis by joining the lip between the otomy and the posterior staple lines. The posterior inner row is being sutured with precise placement of stitches, aided by good exposure of the surgical field.

The surgeon rocks between left and right hands to start the canal over the top, initiating the second inner suture for the anterior row. Large bites are taken around the near corner, sewing out with the left hand, forming a canal with small bites of seromuscular tissue and some mucosa, extending across to the other suture.

The surgeon sews around the near corner, taking large bites and switching to the left hand. The inner anterior row is formed by cannelling over the top with small bites of seromuscular tissue, occasionally catching mucosa for hemostasis. The stitches are tied together, completing the anterior row of a two-layer anastomosis. The original suture is used to imbricate over the staple lines, finalizing the two-layer anastomosis.

The surgeon ties the stitches together to complete a two-layer anastomosis, using an imbricating lumbar suture over the staple lines. The mucosa is caught for hemostasis, although it does not add strength to the anastomosis.
